summary: Jeff Atwood reviews '19 Deadly Sins of Software Security,' a book he unexpectedly found valuable despite receiving it as a conference freebie. He summarizes all 19 security vulnerabilities with CVE database counts, demonstrating that while C/C++ bear responsibility for buffer overflows and related issues, 16 of the 19 sins apply to all languages and platforms. Atwood emphasizes that security usability is the most interesting and challenging sin, as security inherently adds complexity that conflicts with user experience. He concludes that the book serves as an excellent cross-platform, language-agnostic primer on software security risks.
key_points: Only 3 of the 19 security sins are specific to C/C++; the other 16 affect developers on all platforms and languages
SQL injection (2,225), XSS (2,996), and buffer overflows (3,326) represent the most common vulnerability categories in the CVE database
Security usability is the most challenging problem because security inherently conflicts with ease of use
Many developers underestimate risks like network traffic exposure, weak password systems, and improper file access
The book provides practical, language-agnostic guidance rather than deep dives into specific security areas
money_quotes: Usability is tough under the best of conditions – and security is the worst of conditions, at least from the user's perspective.
Security only works if the secure way also happens to be the easy way.
It's true that C and C++ have a heavy cross to bear. But only 3 of the 19 sins can be completely lumped on the plate of K&R.
The other 16 apply almost everywhere, to any developer writing code on any platform. It's a sobering thought.
Most programmers underestimate the risk of transmitting data over the network, even if that data is not private.
Unfortunately, writing properly concurrent code is incredibly difficult.
key_insight: Software security vulnerabilities are overwhelmingly language-agnostic problems, and the hardest challenge isn't technical—it's making security usable enough that users will actually follow secure practices.
